title: SGLang NPU Triton kernel bridge-adaptor — disable broken stub with if False: fallback
problem_description: sgl_kernel_npu.mem_cache.allocator.alloc_extend_kernel is a stub that returns a plain Python function instead of a real Triton kernel. When the sglang runtime tries to invoke it with Triton grid-launch syntax alloc_extend_kernel[(bs,)], the stub raises TypeError (plain function, not subscriptable). The real CUDA Triton kernel works, but the NPU Triton backend hasn't yet caught up. This is a recurring pattern: sgl_kernel_npu modules contain placeholder stubs for kernels that only have CUDA Triton implementations, and the runtime doesn't know to skip them.
rough_fix_approach: Use the if False: bridge-adaptor pattern to permanently disable the broken kernel path while preserving the original code for future reference. The pure-Python alloc_extend_naive fallback already exists as the production path for num_new_pages >= 200, so this is extending a proven fallback to cover all cases. Add # NOTE(seam): comment documenting why the kernel is disabled and what error it would cause. This keeps the original code intact for when the NPU Triton backend matures.

involved_code_files.role: SGLang NPU hardware backend — page allocator that optionally uses sgl_kernel_npu Triton kernels for KV-cache memory management (control-plane, not compute)

reasoning: The sgl_kernel_npu stub-vs-real-kernel pattern is endemic across the sglang NPU backend. Any sgl_kernel_npu module that wraps a Triton kernel may be a stub on Ascend NPU because the NPU Triton backend lags CUDA Triton. The if False: bridge pattern is clean, reversible (the original code stays in the file for reference), and produces a clear NOTE(seam): comment for traceability. The pattern generalizes beyond allocators to any sgl_kernel_npu module where a pure-Python fallback exists. Verified working on Ascend 910B2C with CANN 8.1.RC1.
